title: 2015/12/04 - ENV (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Environment Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for the Environment (Continued) [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for the Environment (Continued)
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Environment Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for the Environment (Continued) Hearing of evidence: Ministry for the Environment James Palmer, Secretary for the Environment and Chief Executive Anne Haira, Deputy Secretary, Partnerships, Investment and Enablement Gina Rangi, Deputy Secretary, Tūmatakōkiri Laura Dixon, Deputy Secretary, Business Transformation & Services Nadeine Dommisse, Deputy Secretary, Environmental Management and Adaptation Natasha Lewis, Deputy Secretary, Strategy Stewardship and Performance Sam Buckle, Deputy Secretary, Climate Change Mitigation and Resource Efficiency Arun Patel, General Manager, Finance and Procurement Simon King, Chief Advisor, Office of the Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for the Environment (Continued). Ministry for the Environment. James Palmer, Secretary for the Environment and Chief Executive

chunks.text: All right. There we go. Sure. And we'll just, um, pop back on to public session. So welcome back, everyone, uh, for our second half of, of the session. Uh, just looking to our structured agenda, we are back on environmental reporting and outcomes, and so we will start with a question from Grant Cullen. Right. Yeah, just the biodiversity on private land and so forth.

Mm-hmm. Thank you. There you go, right. Um, uh, speaking as a farmer and with, with a number of, um, blocks of bush on our property, and the... obviously the QE II Trust does, uh, a lot of work in this space. I think they've got about 200,000 hectares under covenant at the moment, which is, I think, the second-largest equivalent to a national park in the country.

Mm-hmm. What, what... r- how... when you, um, assess the biodiversity, how do, how do you see that, um... the m- the, uh, uh, contribution of that, um, and, and the, and the level of protection that that's provided? In the last year, we've undertaken work around, uh, nature credit markets, and we have ten pilots around New Zealand at the moment on different, uh, la- property types that are looking at, uh, how, uh, it may be possible to, uh, monetize or provide incentives around biodiversity protection on, uh, on private

land, um, uh, or, uh, biodiversity enhancement. So, um, that, that work is intended to inform future decision-making around the role of government in, uh, promoting, uh, nature markets, natural repair markets in particular. Um, and the reform of the resource management system will present some opportunity to support that too, and there'll be further detail, uh, of that announced, uh, in the reform package to government Good.

Mm-hmm. There's a supple. Okay, Lan, what's up? So why the ten pilots? What... like, are they other people's pilots that are sort of under one umbrella, or are they- Y-... your pilots? No. So, um, the, uh, the starting position is that, uh, there is a really, uh, some activity occurring where, um, uh, landowners, private landowners are being funded by third parties to undertake, uh, activity on their land. Um, a, uh, member pr- previous referred to, uh, the QE II Trust. There's a range of others, uh, that are investing in, in that activity. So the government doesn't wanna come in

and crowd that out or necessarily create a single market in the way that we have with the Emissions Trading Scheme for nature credits, reflecting that there's the opportunity for innovation, and there will be a range of different ways in which people will want to, uh, enter into those arrangements. So the point of the pilots is to explore different types of arrangements between private landowners and investors, uh, and, uh, learn from each of those.

So, um, the arrangement we have with each of those, which are existing projects- Mm-hmm.... uh, is that in exchange for some funding support from the ministry, um, we get a- access to the learnings from, uh, from their, um, their, their pilot projects. Okay. Thank you. Uh, we'll now move to Priyanka. Um, mine could actually well be a sup on this.

Um, so- so are you then saying that the different pilots that you have looking at the options of arrangements or other options, um, like QE2 covenants but not necessarily that, uh, will then feed into the advice on the RM reform work? Is that what you're saying?

So the... It's feeding into ad- advice separately around the role of government in, uh, nature repair markets. Um, what I'm saying is that in addition to that- Mm-hmm....resource management reforms will, uh, create the opportunity for mechanisms around, uh, biodiversity, uh, to be, um, uh- uh, funded on private, uh, land- Okay.

...either by local government or potentially as part of offsetting. Right. But that's all detail to- Sure....to cover. So the first part of that then in terms of the other package of options, are there... Is there any information that you can share with the committee on what some other options could be other than QE2 covenants?

Do you want to take that, Anne? Sure. I might just take us back a- a few steps. Um, uh, morien everybody. My name's Anne Hider. Uh, so the origins of the voluntary nature credit markets work actually began in the previous administration, uh, where we consulted on, uh, the proposal for a biodiversity credits market.

Mm-hmm. Uh, and that work was subsequently picked up by, uh, the new Associate Minister for the Environment, uh, Andrew Hoggard. Uh, in June of, uh, this year, we announced a- a number of potential roles that the government could take in helping to enable a voluntary market to scale. Now, the four roles that we outlined firstly were, uh, direction setting.

So we heard very strongly from the market that h- the government being clear on what role it would play or could play in the market needs to be spelled out. It can't just be implicit. So we had to be quite explicit about this is the role we think the government could take. Uh, the second was how could government support, uh, potentially through standard setting, um, the estab- or the establishment of integrity principles? Good projects is probably a simple way of thinking about it. So how do you ensure that the projects happening around New Zealand that might

be part of a voluntary market- Mm-hmm....are good projects? And we can do that through integrity principles. Uh, thirdly, um, how can we help ensure that, uh, investors can trust that the environmental gains that are being articulated by project proponents are real? So an assurance type process. Uh, and then fourthly and finally, promoting transparency, uh, for integrity and accountability.

You could do that, for example, through a national registry, uh, similar to what we have, uh, for the ETS. So fundamentally, what we are asking, uh, market actors and other interested parties to tell us is what role could government take to help enable the market to scale? Mm-hmm. And at a practical level, uh, we tested that through a range of pilots. We are testing it through a range of pilots.

Uh, there are some in Northland, uh, there's an ewe restoration initiative up there, through to a wetlands initiative in Southland, across a wide range of different stakeholder groups including Silver Fern Farms. Uh, and it's really trying to get our head around the role that government can play as an enabler. Mm-hmm. So we don't want to crowd out private market activity.

Uh, we also want to understand, yeah, where we can add the most value, uh, and also provide, um, opportunities for a wide range of projects, smaller projects, uh, through to a- a range of diverse participants. So, uh, offshore, uh, and similar type voluntary markets for carbon, it's often the large scale, um, cashed up type projects that are able to access this market. And, um, Minister Hoggard who has been leading this work has been very interested in how to ensure that you get a range of different size of projects. So it might be a few hectares on

a- a farmer's block that is retired. Uh, how might, uh, a property owner like that be able to benefit from this type of regime? Yeah. Cool. Thanks. Just have a sup on- Yeah....um, the... Staying on the topic of biodiversity but slightly different from the private land, um, stuff. I'm just interested, I see that MfE is doing some work around ecosystem typology.

Mm-hmm. Um, and, uh, um, from- from what I understand, it's around consistency of sort of language, how do we define that and so on. Just interested to get a bit more information about that particular project and also how it intersects, if it does, with, um, the bio-invest tool that DOC has been working on as well.

Mm-hmm. Do you want to pick this up? Yeah. Um, thank you for your question. Uh, just recognizing and- and connecting this to our earlier session and the importance of our role in ensuring that standards are- are upheld and are reliable and consistent. And so ensuring that there is a- a typology and- and consistency of- of classification and approach is a really fundamental role that we need to play across different domains and biodiversity is one of those.

And so, um, the piece of work that you have referred to is helping us with that. Mm-hmm. Uh, it's also an important part of our evidence base in consideration of the role of environmental limits in the new system as well. Uh, and we work closely with- uh, with DOC, uh, on those. It's, uh, there is, uh, staff from- from DOC working in partnership with us.

Right. Okay. Good. And Grant had a supplementary on that. Yeah. Uh, just, um, once again the biodiversity space but once again slightly different tangent. The- when the topic of significant nat- nat- national natural areas come up, that really, that creates a lot of-... um, challenge in the, in the, in the, for, for property owners, um, across the country. It certainly gets a lot of emotions going. Do you think the, with the sort of work that's going on with what we, we, like I mentioned with QE2 and the, and the biodiversity credits, that we

can (clears throat) reach potentially a, a space where, where you d- the, the State doesn't have to come and say, "Well, you have to lock that piece up," or, "You have to control that bit of land like this, run it like this"? 'Cause that, that certainly creates a lot of tension with both iwi and, um, and - (coughs)... private land.

Uh, so I, I, I think we're working at, um, both ends of the spectrum in terms of, um, supporting voluntary action- Yeah.... uh, in the ways that Anne described, but also, uh, obviously the, the regulatory framework for, uh, biodiversity, uh, on private land. Mm-hmm. Um, the minister, uh, Associate Minister responsible, uh, Minister Hoggard, uh, did choose to pause, uh, earlier in the government's term the roll-out of further, um, significant natural areas.

And, uh, all of, um, the, the future arrangements around the, uh, regulation for biodiversity on private land has been carried through the resource management reform work, and, uh, there'll be more detail on that announced next week. Okay. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. And a-another supplementary, Rachel? Oh, no, Wenze- Is it primary?

Yeah. Just a quick sup? Oh, okay, quick sup. Uh, just on the pilots, excuse me, um, when would we expect to hear, like, what the outcome is? So, uh, the next step, uh, in the work is to provide advice to Cabinet in March. Uh, and we developed a methodology that we are applying across the 10 projects, which by the way, I, I omitted to mention, include QE2. Uh, and that will inform our advice around, uh, what role the government could take and over what sort of time horizon.

Thank you. And Rachel. Yeah, thank you. Uh, just, so, generally speaking, not, not on this biodiversity topic, but for all your reporting and monitoring, where, what are you doing for Matauranga Maori in this space? You're the one I'm looking at. Thank you for the question. Uh, and, and just recognizing the, the role of, uh, that we have in relation to environmental reporting is about putting information into the hands of decision-makers, and it's really important that we draw on a range of sources of information.

When we use the term evidence, we, uh, include Matauranga Maori as a, as a knowledge source of r- relevance in the consideration of evidence, just as there are other place-based, uh, information sources that are important, uh, to be considered. Uh, and so, uh, as you can see through our environmental reporting series, uh, we often, where are, um, where there is information, uh, that is relevant to be brought up at a national scale, we do what we can.

More often than not when it comes to Matauranga Maori, it's most relevant at a place-based scale. And so- Mm-hmm.... often you see that reflected through the case studies that we produce alongside our environmental reports, which give a, a richer, more localized, um, story of, of what we see in terms of the State pressure and impact. So- Can, can you just give some examples of, of where something is useful, uh, is used in a local place-based scale, and then when you might think that something's relevant nationally?

Yeah. So, um, you can see examples of, um, the application of Matauranga Maori in a number of our different reports, uh, uh, through our Environment, uh, 2025. Can you tell us about one you choose? Yeah. Um, so the, the, um, role of mahinga kai- Mm-hmm.... and understanding of mahinga kai in, in an ecosystem as, as a food source and, uh, and what that can tell us about what we understand of how the environment is changing, uh, and how it's responding to changes around it. So, um, that, uh, knowledge and information

is important to, uh, contribute to, uh, the broader information source, uh, that helps inform decision-making. Thank you. Thank you. I might just jump in and ask a question here, 'cause I'm quite interested in the biodiversity on private land. And I was just wondering, um, with the catchment groups, um, does the Ministry for Environment have... I know it's more MPI, but obviously, um, you know, these are great groups across the country that, uh, you know, volunteering time to, uh, protect the waterways, uh, across the

private land that we see across New Zealand. And, you know, farmers are doing an amazing job. Um, d- like, what's, wha- how does the Ministry for Environment feed into that? Because you most probably... It's, it's, it's a good source to try and power up to, to help protect our waterways. Pass to Anne for it. (clears throat) Uh, alongside, uh, MPI, um, the Ministry for the Environment has been funding catchment groups, uh, for a number of years, principally, uh, th- recently through Jobs for Nature, through the Freshwater Improvement Fund.

Uh, it is certainly an area that we, um, uh, would be supportive of continuing, uh, to fund. It's something we are contemplating as part of the implementation of freshwater farm plans. Mm-hmm. Uh, we see catchment groups as, uh, being an, an incredible partner. Uh, they unlock a number of, uh, channels and bring tools and skills and capabilities- Mm-hmm.

... that are fundamentally important to achieving the environmental outcomes that we're seeking. So, um, absolutely on board with what you're saying. And do you see the catchment groups, uh, getting better and stronger and, and, and seeing better outcomes? There's a wide variety ac- across, uh, the country but yes, in answer to your question. Uh, they are structured and formed in different ways. So one thing we've been looking at, uh, with MPI is, uh, potential different models for how we might have a nationally consistent approach, uh, to how we fund.

That doesn't mean everyone has to be structured the same, but, uh, we, uh, as- Sorry to go a step back. As part of the model for delivering Jobs for Nature, it involved multiple agencies. So you had DOC, LINZ, MFE, MPI, um, uh, all with slightly different variations of their approach to catchment group funding- Mm-hmm.

Uh, and one of the lessons we took from, uh, the evaluation report at the end of Jobs for Nature, uh, was the importance of having a national, uh, nationally consistent standard in metrics, uh, across these sorts of things. So we can roll up the data and really know whether the approach in, for a catchment group in Southland, um, is comparable to perhaps other (audio cuts out) .

Mm-hmm. So just one last question on this. Uh, so do you see real value in that kind of private-public partnership essentially across the catchment groups across New Zealand in terms of the outcomes? It's... Yes. It's an- It's actually fundamental to how we think about our work in partnering generally- Mm-hmm.

... at the Ministry for the Environment. You know, we are acutely aware, uh, that we are only part of the broader system, uh, that we are not operating generally at the front line of delivery. Uh, so we work very closely with partners, whether that's, uh, local government or catchment groups or EPA or others. Uh, we know that in order to be successful, we need to partner effectively.

Yeah, thank you. And Rachel's got a supplement. Yeah. A couple of sups. Um, one is just to confirm the funding that we were just talking about, that a lot of the catchment groups were funded by Jobs for Nature. Mm-hmm. And that funding's gone. And so now you're looking at whether there's gonna be future government investment in catchment groups.

Yep. But just to- just to clarify though, there has been a- a substantial investment in the catchment groups across the country was my understanding. To date, Jobs for Nature funding or the program officially ended, uh, 30 June. Uh, there are still some projects that are continuing on, uh, but the funding and the program ended then. Um, we... Ministers are aware that there, uh, is somewhat of a gap, I suppose you could call it, that needs to be addressed and there are conversations underway to look at that.

No decisions have been taken yet- (clears throat) Uh, but we are looking at whether, um, a potential part of that solution and approach could be through the implementation of freshwater farm packs. But we did- Just su- We did fund catchment groups. The... Sorry, Ryan, what was- T- To your point though, we did fund catchment groups. Yes, that's right. Uh, but it... That might not have been- (laughs) And actually-... true with the Ministry for Environment.

Oh. Was that true, um, Ministry of Primary Industries- Multiple agencies, yeah. Multiple agencies, yeah. So I suppose that's where perhaps there could be some confusion as to where that funding's- Correct.... all coming from because it's across multiple agencies. That's right. Yeah. It was under Jobs for Nature a multi-agency approach. One of the key questions considered is should we have a single agency to fund catchment groups or a single way of being able to draw that information together so that there is a single

source of truth. Mm-hmm. But then recently there was a, um, a national body of catchment groups that was established. Yes, so they- they have been- And they were obviously, uh, given some funding to, uh, facilitate the catchment groups across the country was my understanding. Actually, o- one-off funding provided and there's further work underway that Anne refers to about, uh, from Budget '26 onwards.

What does the, what does the forward funding look? We do have a multi-year contract with, uh, the New Zealand Landcare Trust who provide, um, facilitation and coordination around catchment groups as well. So that, that remains an ongoing commitment at this stage. Um, but that's also time-limited. Okay. No, that's good to understand, uh, that there obviously is still funding there available.

S- And I had another supple- Alana might be more- Lan. Yes. Sorry. Yeah. Just supplementary just while we're on the execs thing. So to help us understand the gap as you call it, so Jobs for Nature was $1.2 billion and supported over 10,000 jobs. And you note in your report it created enduring benefits for biodiversity, freshwater, and regional economies. And you talk about its legacy continuing through strengthened community capability and ongoing partnerships. What is the funding that has been provided? A- Accepting that it might be across different

sources, do you have a ballpark figure on what that amount of funding is for catchment groups? Not at this point. Um, just to refer back to James's comment, uh, the forward solution for funding catchment groups is under active consideration and thinking about what that model might look like, taking into account the group that was referred to earlier, um, a catchment collective group.

So the design specifically for funding catchment groups is under active consideration. But wasn't there a specific funding amount in the previous budget? Mm-hmm. There was. Do you know what that was? I don't think so- I can't remember. But I don't think we've got that off the top of our heads. But- Okay. But yes, there was... There was funding provided for, uh, uh, the current financial year.

I think Budget 2025 committed 36 million over four years for the catchment groups. Okay. Yeah, the- the three EPIs. Three EPIs. So not three... That which was exactly what we were talking about. So we're kind of like talking about like a one point- Yes, that's that, that's- Um- That's the $9 million a year. Mm-hmm. Between 1.2 billion Jobs for Nature and about 34 million, did you say?

Yeah, in a COVID rundown- But that wasn't specifically Jobs for Nature. Um, the, the catchment groups. The 1.2 billion covered all of Jobs for Nature, that covered- Mm-hmm. Yeah, that's right.... a whole range of- Absolutely.... (overlapping dialogue) But I'm trying to get the magnitude of the gap that we've just been told exists and is very clear (overlapping dialogue) I've got to make a difference. Um, should we move on to another supplementary from Grant?

(overlapping dialogue) Yeah, yeah, but I'm at- Rachel has a supplement. If you want to, please. If, if you're good on this. Yeah, okay. Because mine's quite... Yeah. Well, mine's more general. It's like (laughs) more general stuff. But w- we're talking about biodiversity on private land. That, that was, um, the Chair's question. And we had another item that we haven't discussed yet, uh, which was managing risks from ETS-driven exotic forestry, particularly permanent forestry.

Mm-hmm. And I just want to ask if you've done... if the Ministry's done much thinking about, um-... ways to incentivize, um, indigenous, uh, forestry in, in terms of the ETS and where that's at and how those two different- Yeah, Sam might want to provide, um, some, some more detail on this.

... But, um, certainly there has been a, um, further work done on, uh, updating, uh, the lookup tables, uh, for, um, indigenous, uh- Mm-hmm. Yeah.... afforestation under Emissions Trading Scheme. Um, and it's work that we do with the MPI around procuring the science to support that. So that is a, that's an ongoing, uh, uh, process. Um, there's been further policy work done around the removals framework for, um, Emissions Trading Scheme in, under the Climate Change Response Act. So, uh, for removals that might either

be, uh, assured for voluntary, uh, purposes or under the ETS itself. And so looking at the ability for the Climate Change Response Act to support a wider range of activities that, uh, that might be, um, eligible for removals. So like Australia. So, so for example, wetlands being, uh, able to be accounted for, uh, for, um, either for voluntary carbon or for, uh, ETS units. So there's a, so there, there's been policy work done on that.

Right. There are some legislative, uh, changes that will need to come through, uh, CCRA Amendment Bill, uh, to be, uh, yet introduced to the house. So yes, it has been a long area, area of work. So you are doing that, thinking. Anything else, Sam, you want to add on that? No, we, I think we provided the committee with... I think you asked for our, our views on the issue of, um, forestry within the ETS.

And we provided some thoughts there on the wider issue, not so much specifically on natives. I, I, I don't think there's any specific work at the moment, um, looking at... We haven't been s- you know, advice that's been sought around, you know, playing with the ETS framework to, uh, incentivize somehow, uh, natives, um, over and above the extent to which you get rewarded for them at the moment.

Okay. Those are choices that governments could take down the line if, if they wanted to. There is interest in, you know, taking advantage of Crown land and, um, opportunities to plant native trees on, on Crown land and that's sort of a work in progress. Just native trees? And, and is Crown land there, is that all conservation land?

Uh, so, uh, so there are a range of constraints on Crown land. Um, so we've provided advice on, uh, opportunities for afforestation across both, um, uh, native and, and exotic. Right. Um, uh, there's yet to be announcements on, uh, that. So that's something that's been under active consideration by, uh, ministers.

Mm-hmm. Um, and we've, um, we have identified that there is constraints that exist in terms of, um, the purpose for which land is held. Oh, definitely. Yeah. Understood. And Grant? Yeah, I'm sorry. Um, going back to the catchment groups, if that's all right. Mm-hmm. New- probably New Zealand's some l-... It's okay. I didn't realize. I thought Julian was- Jumping around a little New Zealand's largest, probably largest funded catchment group, if want to put it that way, is Kaipara Mine and Remediation.

I'd be curious to know what's, what's your, what's your feedback? How it's progressing? I'd love to... You know, as a Northland MP, it's quite a big part of my patch. (sighs) It has a major impact. How's that? Obviously, um, you'll be aware, uh, that they have, uh, recently gone through a change in leadership with, um, Justine, uh, moving on. So that, uh, will be quite significant for them.

And I know she's had a significant impact in the success of the program. Uh, but by and large, uh, they are all on track, uh, and we will continue to fund, uh, them into the future. Yeah. And, and, and in terms of the outcomes, are we seeing some measurable outcomes yet? Yes. And, and what specifically? 'Cause it's obviously the main challenge, as I understand, is around the sediment control and so forth, working with local landowners in particular.

Yes, yes. Uh, pro- uh, I could probably come back to you- Yeah.... with some more detail on this one. But just noting that progress with sedimentation does, uh- Take a while. ... normally take some time, uh, to demonstrate. But if that, uh, um, chair is comfortable with this- Yes.... this was- Finding more, finding some further information, that would be great. Yeah.

This was a case study in one of our recent reports, so we can send that- That would be great. Thank you, yeah.... to that effect to you. Can I just build, in terms of the, the role of catchment groups, I just wanted to connect it back to our conversations about data and the role- Yeah. ... of the Minister for the Environment. So we've got some work underway to develop a, a tiered data framework that can ensure that there can be standards in place to ensure confidence in place-based data that's collected so it can be

used, um, to inform decision-making where appropriate. So ensuring that the checks and balances are in place, but also recognizing that, uh, data that's informing, uh, catchment scale decisions- Yeah.... uh, is different than data that, that comes in at a national level. So we're working through a, a, a tiered framework. We're just at the early stages of that.

Mm-hmm. Uh, that of course links back to the conversation about other place-based data sources. So ensuring that- Yeah, and does- And actually I, I was just thinking on that data because does that mean that it's easier to track information across multiple agencies? Because often that can be the challenge, right? Is that the, as you say, the, the funding and the work is happening across multiple agencies within government.

And so does that data help with, with that and the tracking performance? So one of the critical challenges that we have with environmental information, which you would have heard from the Parliamentary Commissioner- Yeah, we do. Mm-hmm. ... a number of times, is the interoperability between data sets. They- Mm-hmm. So the ability to tell a... to pull together a picture because often the data is, uh, collected with different methodologies and approaches. And so that's why that our role in producing data standards and

ensuring that methodologies can be consistent is so critical to ensure that there's better data and more data available in the system to inform decision-making at all levels. Yep. And j- just in terms of data, one of the feedbacks I get from, from catchment groups is that they often have to... in terms of monitoring and measuring outcomes, they find that they, that the regional councils-...

ha- ha- do limited work in that space, versus the, the data they require is much more detailed and they do, some of them do it themselves to, 'cause they wanna be able to demonstrate what's happen- actually happening. Um, and is there work going on in that space to try and actually deliver a model that's gonna give us more accurate and more information?

So, so that's exactly the basis of the, the tiered data framework- Yeah.... that I mentioned. So recognizing that data quality needs to reflect the decision, the level of decision-making- Yeah.... and having the checks and balances in place. Because often, at present, citizen science, whether it's created- Yeah.... through catchment groups or other forms, is not recognized, uh- Yeah.

... through into some, some data channels. We've, through environmental reporting, done a lot of work, uh, with sustainable coastlines with the- Oh, great. Yeah.... the marine litter, and so that data can be- Mm-hmm.... uh, brought up and, and utilized at a national level, but it does require the methods and the standards- Yeah.... to be in place so- Yeah.... we can have confidence in the data, but recognize the flexibility that's required.

Mm-hmm. Because the data to inform a catchment level decision- Mm-hmm.... is different than, than if you were looking at a, at a national level decision. And so the, the data points that might be available- Yeah.... uh, at a catchment level differ than, than what's regionally. So that's the point of that work. And just, and one other final question, 'cause the firs- one of the, I get given examples where they, um, the local original council might be measuring water quality at a lower level of the catchment, yet if you go right back to the

start of the catchment, sometimes that, the, the challenge can actually be starting there. The data, like for example, whether it's measuring a, a, a particular data point- Mm-hmm.... it actually might be worse at the start because of naturally occurring phenomena in the, in the water quality. And, and 'cause, and that's one of the things that really frustrates them because, and they feel they're up against it.

So, is that gonna be part of making sure we get that accurate full picture of a, of, of a catchment, not just the bottom end? Mm-hmm. That's like, this is obviously a complex and multi-year, uh- Yeah.... piece of work. Yeah. Uh, and recognizing, uh, that we need to have a monitoring network for the country that- Mm-hmm.

... that reflects, uh, different decision-making levels but also reflects a robust regime that we can rely on. Yeah. Uh, and so this does require investment. It requires investment over time, uh, to ensure that, that some of those fundamental challenges can be addressed. Uh, you can read any one of our environment reports, uh, that have a summary of, of, uh, our perspective in terms of the data gaps and the challenges that exist. We're, we're absolutely, uh, working hard to address what we can, and we see that as fundamental to our role-

Yeah.... sitting here, uh, to ensure that as lead advisors actually ensuring that there can be confidence in data and that data can be accessible. There's, there's no point in data just being there if it's not accessed as well. So fundamental to our job- Mm-hmm. ... and our role ahead. Thank you. Appreciate it.
